---
title: "Week 6 -- Facilitator Notes"
subtitle: "Exploratory Data Analysis II: ggplot2 and Visual Thinking"
---

## Learning Objectives

Residents should move from describing distributions with numbers (Week 5) to seeing them with pictures. They must understand that visualization is not decoration -- it is a diagnostic and communication tool. The key skill is choosing the right geometry and aesthetics for the clinical question, then refining the design for clarity and accuracy.

---

## Session Format

| Segment | Duration | Activity |
|---------|----------|----------|
| Recap | 5 min | Review Week 5 summaries; how would you show the same information visually? |
| Lecture | 20 min | Grammar of graphics; mapping vs. setting; geometry selection |
| Live Demo | 15 min | Build MAP time series; overlay, facet, annotate |
| Independent Exercise | 30 min | Parts 1-5: create data, plot, facet, annotate, highlight hypotension |
| Group Discussion | 15 min | Part 6: compare dashboards; debate which panel answers which question |
| Reflection | 5 min | Export best figure; discuss publication standards |
| Check-in | 5 min | Preview Week 7 (statistical testing); verify GitHub push |

---

## Key Teaching Points

### ggplot2 Is Not Magic -- It Is a Grammar

Many residents have used Excel or GraphPad and think of plotting as "choose chart type." ggplot2 forces a different mindset: data -> aesthetics -> geometry -> refinement.

Walk through the template explicitly:

> "Every plot starts with data. Then you decide: what goes on the x-axis? On the y-axis? What color? What shape? Those decisions go inside aes(). Then you pick the mark: point, line, box, bar. That is the geom. Then you adjust scales, facets, and themes."

**Common confusion:** aes() means "map a variable to a visual property." It does NOT mean "set a property to a constant."

```r
# WRONG: aes(color = "red") maps the string literal "red" to color
ggplot(data, aes(x = time, y = map, color = "red")) + geom_line()

# RIGHT: color = "red" sets a constant color for all lines
ggplot(data, aes(x = time, y = map)) + geom_line(color = "red")
```

**Diagnostic question:** If a resident gets a legend with a single entry called "red," they put the color inside aes().

### Grouping Is Essential for Time Series

The most common error in this exercise: forgetting group = patient_id.

Without grouping, ggplot2 connects all points in order, creating a single zigzag line that jumps between patients. With grouping, each patient gets their own line.

Demonstrate both:

```r
# WRONG: one continuous line jumping between patients
ggplot(map_data, aes(x = time_min, y = map)) + geom_line()

# RIGHT: one line per patient
ggplot(map_data, aes(x = time_min, y = map, group = patient_id)) + geom_line()
```

This connects directly to the long-format data structure from Week 3. Each row is one observation; the grouping variable tells ggplot2 which observations belong together.

### Facets vs. Overlays: When to Use Which

| Goal | Use | Why |
|------|-----|-----|
| Compare overall patterns | Overlay (alpha transparency) | Shows density and common trajectory |
| Compare individual details | Facet | Avoids overplotting; reveals outliers |
| Compare subgroups | Color + overlay | Direct comparison of group means |
| Compare many individuals | Facet with small multiples | Scales fixed for comparability |

**Teaching point:** The overlaid plot (Part 2) shows that most patients follow a similar pattern but obscures individual variation. The faceted plot (Part 3) reveals individual responses but makes it harder to see the group pattern. Neither is wrong -- they answer different questions.

### Clinical Annotation Is Not Decoration

The event annotations (Part 4) and hypotension shading (Part 5) connect the plot to clinical reality. A MAP trace without event labels is just a squiggly line. With labels, it becomes: "Intubation caused a pressor response in 7/10 patients."

Key annotations for clinical time series:
- **Vertical lines:** Mark events (intubation, incision, drug administration)
- **Horizontal lines:** Mark thresholds (MAP = 65, SpO2 = 92%, glucose = 70)
- **Shaded regions:** Highlight abnormal periods (hypotension, hypoxemia)
- **Text labels:** Name events directly on the plot

**Rule:** Every annotation must answer a clinical question. If it does not, remove it.

### Color Is a Scientific Choice, Not an Aesthetic One

Default ggplot2 colors are not colorblind-safe. The default discrete palette uses red and green, which 8% of male clinicians cannot distinguish.

Teach the viridis family:

```r
# Colorblind-safe sequential (continuous data)
ggplot(data, aes(x = time, y = map, color = cardiac_output)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_viridis_c()

# Colorblind-safe qualitative (groups)
ggplot(data, aes(x = time, y = map, color = treatment_group)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_viridis_d()
```

**Clinical red flag:** If you use red and green on the same plot, a reviewer with deuteranopia cannot read your figure. Use blue-orange or viridis instead.

### Publication-Ready Means Reproducible and Accessible

A figure is publication-ready when:
- It is generated by code, not hand-adjusted in PowerPoint
- It uses vector graphics (PDF, SVG) for print resolution
- It has sufficient DPI (300+) for raster outputs
- It includes alt text for screen readers
- It uses colorblind-safe palettes
- It has readable fonts at final print size

```r
ggsave(
  filename = "figure_1_map_trajectories.pdf",
  width = 180,          # mm for single-column journal
  height = 120,
  units = "mm"
)
```

**Rule:** Never export from RStudio plot pane with right-click "Save as PNG." Always use ggsave() with explicit dimensions.

---

## Live Demo Script

Build the MAP plot together, step by step:

1. Generate the data (Part 1) -- explain set.seed(42), expand.grid(), long format
2. Basic line plot (Part 2) -- show the jumbled version first, then add group
3. Facet (Part 3) -- compare fixed vs. free scales
4. Annotations (Part 4) -- add vertical lines, discuss readability
5. Hypotension shading (Part 5) -- explain geom_rect() with -Inf and Inf
6. Dashboard (Part 6) -- show how different views answer different questions
7. Export (Part 7) -- demonstrate PDF vs. PNG, dimensions, DPI

**Key pause:** After the basic line plot, ask: "What is wrong with this picture?" (Answer: lines jump between patients because group is missing.)

**Key pause:** After faceting, ask: "Why are all y-axes the same?" (Answer: scales = "fixed" enables comparison. If one patient is hypotensive and another is not, you see it immediately.)

---

## Exercise Guidance

**Part 1:** Data generation. Watch for:
- library(dplyr) before using %>% and mutate()
- set.seed(42) for reproducibility
- Understanding expand.grid() creates the Cartesian product
- NA_character_ for missing event labels

**Part 2:** Basic plot. Common issues:
- Forgetting group = patient_id -- show the jumbled plot first, then fix
- alpha = 0.5 for transparency -- explain why without it, the last patient drawn covers the others

**Part 3:** Facets. Common issues:
- ncol = 2 vs. ncol = 5 -- discuss readability
- scales = "free_y" -- show what happens and why fixed is better for comparison

**Part 4:** Annotations. Common issues:
- geom_vline() needs data = event_times because the events are a separate dataset
- geom_text() with y = Inf and vjust = 1.5 -- explain coordinate system

**Part 5:** Hypotension shading. Common issues:
- rle_id() may not exist -- provide the alternative with cumsum() and lag()
- geom_rect() with ymin = -Inf and ymax = Inf -- explain it spans the full y-axis
- Red shading with alpha = 0.1 -- too dark obscures the data; too light is invisible

**Part 6:** Dashboard. Common issues:
- patchwork not installed -- show install.packages("patchwork")
- Panel layout: (p1 + p2) / (p3 + p4) means top row has two panels, bottom row has two panels
- Different datasets for different panels (event_times, hypotension_data)

**Part 7:** Export. Common issues:
- Forgetting units = "in" or units = "mm" -- default is inches
- DPI too low for print -- 72 DPI is screen resolution; 300+ is print resolution
- File format: PNG for web/PowerPoint; PDF for publications

---

## Troubleshooting During Session

| Problem | Quick Fix |
|---------|-----------|
| "My lines are jumbled" | Add group = patient_id inside aes() |
| "Colors are ugly" | Add scale_color_viridis_d() or scale_color_brewer() |
| "Facet labels are cut off" | Increase ggsave() height or use theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 8)) |
| "I cannot install patchwork" | Use gridExtra::grid.arrange() as fallback |
| "geom_rect() shades everything" | Check that hypotension_data has correct start_time and end_time |
| "Text labels overlap" | Use check_overlap = TRUE or ggrepel::geom_text_repel() |
| "Plot looks different when I knit" | ggsave() inside code chunks can conflict with chunk options -- use chunk fig.width and fig.height instead |
| "I do not understand rle_id()" | Use the cumsum() + lag() alternative provided in the exercise |

---

## Assessment

Verify each resident can:

- [ ] Build a ggplot from ggplot() + geom_*() with correct aes() mappings
- [ ] Distinguish mapped aesthetics (inside aes()) from constant settings (outside aes())
- [ ] Choose geom_line(), geom_point(), geom_histogram(), geom_boxplot() appropriately
- [ ] Create faceted plots with facet_wrap() and justify scales = "fixed" vs. "free"
- [ ] Add clinical annotations: geom_hline(), geom_vline(), geom_text()
- [ ] Export figures with ggsave() in appropriate format, resolution, and dimensions
- [ ] Apply colorblind-safe palettes (viridis, ColorBrewer)
- [ ] Combine multiple plots into a dashboard with patchwork

If not, schedule 1-on-1. Week 7 (statistical testing) and Week 8 (Table 1) assume these visualization skills.

---

## Preparation Checklist

- [ ] Tested MAP simulation code on classroom R version
- [ ] Verified patchwork installs cleanly (or prepared gridExtra fallback)
- [ ] Prepared colorblindness simulation (e.g., dichromat package or online simulator)
- [ ] Printed ggplot2 cheat sheets for each resident
- [ ] Identified residents who struggled with dplyr in Week 3-4
- [ ] Verified classroom machines can render Quarto with embedded ggplot2 figures

---

## Connection to Week 5 and Week 7

**From Week 5:** "Last week you computed means and medians. This week you see them. A histogram is the shape behind the summary statistics. A boxplot is the five-number summary drawn."

**To Week 7:** "Next week we ask: is the difference between groups real or random? The plots you draw this week will become the figures that justify your choice of statistical test. A skewed distribution on a histogram means: do not use a t-test. A clear separation on a boxplot means: maybe there is an effect."

End session with: "Numbers describe. Pictures convince. Next week: prove it."
